medications.indication_rationale: The foundation of eczema care — rebuilds the barrier; apply liberally several times daily and after showering; replace soap.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply liberally to the whole body multiple times daily, especially within minutes of showering (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: To active red itchy areas to settle inflammation; source used betamethasone valerate 0.1% BD for 7 days to affected patches. Match potency to site/severity (avoid prolonged potent steroids on the face/thin skin).
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply to active patches twice daily, review at ~7 days (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: A night-time sedating antihistamine can aid sleep and reduce nocturnal scratching during a severe flare — it does NOT treat the eczema itself; source used promethazine 25mg at night.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 25mg orally at night as needed (adult) (as per source note)

medications.drug_name: antihistamine as primary eczema treatment / oral antibiotics without infection
medications.indication_rationale: Non-sedating antihistamines do not treat eczema; antibiotics are not indicated without signs of bacterial infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply thick emollient within ~3 minutes of a lukewarm shower onto damp skin; stop all soaps/bubble baths/scented products; take short lukewarm (not hot) showers.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Locks in moisture and removes irritants that strip the barrier and worsen itch.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the bedroom cooler/humidified; keep nails short; use the sedating antihistamine only at night for the flare.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces nocturnal scratching and heat-driven itch.

patient_education_points.point: This is an eczema flare — your skin barrier is dry and inflamed, which drives the itch-scratch cycle. The mainstays are lots of greasy moisturiser and a steroid cream on the red areas; it should settle over 3–5 days. A drowsy antihistamine at night can help you sleep, but it doesn't treat the eczema.
Moisturise right after a lukewarm shower, stop all soaps and scented products, and keep going with the moisturiser daily even once it settles to prevent relapse.

Seek urgent review / ED if the skin suddenly develops lots of small painful punched-out blisters, weeps yellow fluid or honey-coloured crusts, or you get a fever — these mean infection needing different treatment.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response to TCS; step down and establish maintenance emollient/proactive plan; consider referral if not controlled.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Punched-out blisters/weeping/crusting or fever — urgent review/ED.

must_include_items: Emollient/soap-substitution as the foundation + site-appropriate topical corticosteroid to active areas
Trigger avoidance (soap/fragrance, hot showers, dry heat)
Sedating antihistamine only as a night-time sleep aid (not eczema treatment)
acceptable_alternatives: Any suitable emollient; potency matched to site (e.g. hydrocortisone for face, moderate/potent for limbs)
errors_of_commission: Antihistamine (or antibiotics) as the primary eczema treatment without infection
Prolonged potent topical steroid to the face/thin skin
Advising hot showers / continued scented products
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires emollient + appropriate TCS, trigger avoidance, and a secondary-infection safety-net.
